commentBody: I liked this puzzle a lot. It made me think. And it was tricky to get all the last letters right.

Heads up dark mode users: you may have to go to the light side for this one so that you can more easily see the bold letters. If you want to stay in dark mode, looking at the clues in list mode helped me see the bold letters

It was a little tough for me to identify the bold letters, but eventually I picked them out. The trick was fun and actually made a difference in solving the puzzle.  Also there were quite a few nice clues that took a little time to work out.  I thought using lay as the past tense in LAYLOW was a nice touch, and had me scratching my head for a bit.  MTDOOM flummoxed me for a minute too as I tried Mordor first.  Thanks Adam, it was a lovely puzzle all around.

I think this was the first puzzle in a while that was perfectly appropriate for its day off the week. 

Quality Wednesday puzzle.
I printed the puzzle. No bolds here so I did it as themeless came here to see the joke
6A tripped me up. I thought that was spelled a different way my entire life!

I had trouble distinguishing the bold letters. (Oldoer? Bue? :doggie headtilt:)  Granted I don't have the best eyesight any more, but as it was executed I couldn't solve the themers except by crosses. BUILDABEAR amused me but it made it harder to appreciate the puzzle than the puzzle deserved.

On what planet are Blink-182 and Sum 41 considered “skate rock” bands?? Give me a break.. like yes I get that NOFX, Pennywise, Black Flag, Descendents, Suicidal Tendencies, etc would be too niche for a Wednesday, but this clue is a massive stretch. /soapbox

Overall great puzzle.

45D has a tense mismatch, I think? “TRIED not to draw attention” vs “LAY low”? Shouldn’t it be LIED LOW or a similar change to the clue?
Good puzzle. Lay low (tense is off, no?) and Mt Doom (no signal in clue to use an abbreviation) tripped me up.

Ah well, MORDOR before Mt. Doom. And I get my jibes & gibes confused. And is a bow really a part of a musical instrument? 

Letter between Juliet & Lima got me thinking about that novel by Maria Vargas Llosa, "Aunt Julia and the Script Writer" set in 1950s Lima: satire, memoir, romance all neatly woven together. But then it occurred to me that this was a Nato Alphabet thing, and what a great thing it was, back then, to FORM AN ALLIANCE, but then circumstances and negligence allowed some people to CREATE A MONSTER, and while some make a stink, we're all really bone tired and would rather have a vodka tonic, a candy bar, sake, an oreo... Trotsky must be rolling in his grave to see what his revolution has become!

Speaking of which, a lovely Jamie Ding moment on Jeopardy about a week ago. 

"A piece of mountaineering equipment at the International Spy Museum in D.C. was used to kill this man in 1940."

Ding was the only one to get it right; he wrote:

Who is Trotsky
(ouch!)

I don't know if the whole thing is just whooshing over my head, but I don't get the puzzle at all.  Is the point behind having the clues for the theme answers being bold letters in a long phrase that you are "making" a pact/brute/etc?  The phrase the bold letters are from is REALLY long, so it's not a major construction feat IMO to form four different short words of 4 or 5 letters each out of that long phrase.  

Do the four theme answers have anything to do with each other?   Are they "bold" or something?

This is a perfectly fine puzzle, and if the bold-letter gimmick were removed, this would be a great themeless.  But I'm not understanding the theme, even after reading the constructor notes.

I am very confused.xwo

Brilliant!

Twice I said WOW out loud: when I got the gist of it, and when I read the constructor notes pointing to the word ASSEMBLE in the themers. 
IMHO, that extra layer would have been fantastic.

But even without it, mind blown.

Thank you, Mr. Wagner.
You've rocked it yet again!
Lay low has a tense issue

I haven't memorized the NATO alphabet, but I usually recognize when a clue is making a reference to it, so I look up a chart. Does that mean I'm cheating? Does it make me a bad person? Most importantly, should I care?

Within the span of fifteen minutes I went from seeing SKATEPUNK for the first time, to seeing a video that explained what it was, plus its entire history: when it peaked, what influenced it, how it changed, and what (in this guy's opinion) killed it off.
Fun puzzle! Nice one!

For everyone thinking that there is a tense issue with LAY, this is straight out of MWD:
Lay is often used where lie is considered standard, as in "I'm going to lay down for a quick nap." The use, which dates to the 14th century, troubled no one until the 18th, but since then, people who care about such things have tried to teach the rest of us that a person lies, not lays, down. Lay in the related use means "to place (someone or something) down gently in a flat position." It requires an object; there must be a thing or person being placed. //Lay it down. //It's time to lay the baby down for a nap. Lie, on the other hand, does not require an object; instead, the one lying down is already in position or is moving or going to move to such a position on their own. //The cat lies there every morning. //You can lie down on the sofa. 
The tenses of the verbs complicate things further. Lay becomes laid and laying //She laid the baby in his crib for a nap. //They have laid it all out for you. //I'm laying out the fabric.
Lie becomes lay, lain, and lying: //The baby lay asleep in his crib. //He has lain there since noon. //A note was lying on the table when I came home.

On a separate note, Pennywise is a SKATE PUNK local band right here in my neck of the woods. Jim Lindberg is heavily involved in the Beachlife Festival here in town which is an annual three day concert. This year’s headliners for each night are Duran Duran (Friday), The Offspring (Saturday) and James Taylor (Sunday)

Got fooled a couple places—Smooth instead of SERENE, and MordOr before MT DOOM—before getting corrected via crosses.

But then I expect some of that when we get to midweek. And it was fun to have to do a little "work" to decipher the clues for the themers. Prep for Thursday?

Fun puzzle, Adam, and thanks!

I just barely escaped.

I had FORMAL ALLIANCE for 43A, and SKATEPULK seemed like a reasonably unreasonable name for a rock genre.

I thought I'd change the ...PULK to ...PUNK and see what happened, and what happened was I got the happy music.

I don't think I really deserved it, though.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: That was so crunchy for a Wednesday I think I chipped a tooth.

Theme felt cryptic-like, nice. 

Go Gunners! Let’s go to Budapest!!!!!!🏆❤️🤍

So this was MUCH more impressive than it seems at first glance. When I saw the first themer, I was a little bit “ho-hum”. But then when I realized it was the same clue each time, and how natural and elegant the entries were with phrases related to making/forming etc., I was wowed. (BUILDABEAR was my favorite). And thanks Isaac for giving us a little insight into how the sausage was made. Terrific decision to keep it to four themers, because this was a very interesting, chewy grid for a Wednesday and would have been fun even without the theme.
Some clever clues like “Veg out in a spa” and “paper view” and even a fresh OREO clue. I liked the ASAHI and SAKE cross and the “Brest friends” near ABREAST, not to mention TROTS and TROTSKY. . 
Happy to say I got THEDEETS with little prompting, I guess crosswords are making me cooler. I like the little misdirect of MORDOR before MTDOOM. Last, I always love a crossword coincidence, and I just got back from a wedding where I shared CAKE with a former member of the Village People, who shared his YMCA moves with us during the disco party the night before. You can’t beat that!

Much more difficult than recent Wednesdays, which is not a bad thing. My last stumbling block was 6A, because the down clues seemed to have a tenuous grasp on their respective entries. GO TIME as a moment of truth rather than a moment of action? EEK as a synonym for Holy Cannoli? GIBE spelled with a G?

I’m not complaining, I’m sure it’s all fine. It just felt a little klunky.

As I was solving, a few random thoughts popped up:
-How long has it been since we’ve had a rebus? I feel like we’re overdue.
-How do the Village People feel about their anthem being used as propaganda? And why was that the chosen song? I’ve never understood. But I’d be happy to never think about it again. (If future constructors need to include an entry from the Village People catalog, may I suggest Macho Man? Equally ridiculous, less triggering.)
-BUILD A BEAR from “Pooh” made me smile. But I’m a sucker for the Hundred Acre Wood.

We often see comments about being on the same wavelength as a puzzle or constructor, which makes solving a breeze and angels are heard on high. I was so far off today I thought I would be done in…on a Wednesday. EEK! Something finally clicked, but CREATE A MONSTER summed it up for me. I haven’t read all comments yet but if it’s all about how easy this was I think I’ll LAY LOW until Thursday.
I’ve finished solving and gotten my gold star, but I’m still stumped! Could someone please help me understand how the clue “Suit, e.g.” is connected to the answer?

FYI, the letter between India and Kilo is Juliett, with two T's.  It's spelled that way as a compromise between Juliet, in which French-speakers read the T as silent, and Juliette, which might entice the rest of the world to venture a fourth syllable.

FORM AN ALLIANCE. 
Anyone else sick of "make a deal"?
CUKE in a spa? pretty stretchy, IYAM.

On my phone the bold letters were nearly indistinguishable from the others. Made it very difficult. 

Hated the clue for GIBE, way too obscure for me, perhaps I’m too obtuse. 

Otherwise I enjoyed it.

I think it’s extremely impressive that four very common phrases were all debuted with the use of a single clue. 

Once I caught onto the theme it made solving that much easier. Fun!

Very clever, Adam.
The first 3 themers are all (to me) natural, somewhat common phrases, and I was wondering what the 4th was doing there. TIL Build-A-Bear
So fresh.

I'm a ten-month newbie and today is my first Wednesday without any lookups. Yay!! Today's Midi has 13A ["Winnie-the-Pooh" author] solving to AAMILNE. And today's Crossword has 57A [(P)ut all the b(o)ld letters in this clue t(o)get(h)er?] with "Pooh" in bold letters, solving to BUILDABEAR. It looks like some people like to FORMANALLIANCE?!? Disney's animated "Winnie the Pooh" is all over the internet. For a real treat, there are also the original beautifully illustrated books I read as a child: " Winnie the Pooh," "The House at Pooh Corner," "When we Were Very Young," and so on. https://www.amazon.com/Winnie-Pooh-Milne/dp/0525444432

Nice crossword, but I didn’t feel the clues for 44-down and 45-down matched their answers…

For 44-down MTDOOM I would have had an abbreviation in the clue, e.g. ‘Frodo’s destination in LOTR’
(I mistakenly had MORDOR as the answer for this) 

For 45-down LAYLOW I didn’t feel the tense of the clue matched the answer… instead of ‘tried not to draw attention’ I would have had ‘Try not to draw attention’

The regular pdf version* did not have the emboldened letters which made for quite the challenge, in a good way.  Top center a real STINKer.  Nice one.  

*Newspaper version had the enhanced clues.
That was fun

This puzzle could have been nice but since the bold letters don’t show up well enough to be differentiated from normal letters on the iPhone app it was a total failure for me — an uninteresting theme less with lots of lousy short fill.   NYT  programmers ought to do better and there  ought to have people who check this stuff but obviously there aren’t.

My preferred print version didn't show the bold letters, so that presented an extra layer to solve which made this a little tougher and more entertaining. 

Clever, with themers very much in the language.  That must have been tricky to accomplish with the same clue for all four.

Adam always impressess.

I’m sitting here, shaking my head in amazement, looking at this theme.  How did Adam come up with it?

A theme involving capital letters in repeated clues that make a word suggested by the answers to those clues – where does this come from?  This is not low hanging fruit.  This comes from a highly creative mind.  

Then came the skill of fleshing out this theme.  Coming up with a clue from which the four suggested words could be drawn.  Coming up with four theme answers that fit the same pattern, that fit puzzle symmetry.  So, mix high skill in there with the high creativity. 

And here’s something else coming from such a mind – a new clue for OBOE, a word appearing in the Times puzzle more than 800 times.  An angle none of the inventive bunch of Crosslandia constructors ever used before.

You rock, Adam.  You bring meteoric spark to the box, and thank you a splendid outing with a generous helping of amazement.

Not a happy solver tonight. First, I always print the .pdf version, unless I see on the .pdf before I print that I should probably be using the newspaper version. Nothing on that grid gave me a hint. So when I started to solve the puzzle, it was a mystery. That made it hard. I realized that, in all likelihood, there were bolded letters in other versions.

But there were also a number of things that were way beyond my pay grade, including the entire top center. MT DOOM and SKATE PUNK were also unknown to me.

The fact that I managed to complete the puzzle without any lookups is nothing short of a miracle, but I had to look at the completed grid to see that my guesses in the top middle were correct. Needless to say, I did not like this puzzle, and I think it's not the first time that I didn't like a puzzle by him. I seem to be outnumbered by most here. Oh well.
Could someone explain 8D to me? I have no idea what it means haha

Clever with a crunch!
Loved it.


True story-
yesterday I spent 57 hours on hold
in between 14 brief, confusing conversations with
a lovely girl whose English is *not* fluent.

The issue 
( and I swear I am not joking )
was her struggling to understand 
my *name.*

My pathetic ability to recall
the NATO alphabet
( not that it would have helped much )
sounded like this-

“ No, miss, that’s not my name either.
I’ll try again-

C as in…catastrophic…?
A as in…apostrophe..?”

At one point I said 
“N…as in…Neanderthal…”

to a poor girl who couldn’t understand
the numbers in my zip code.

So, that was fun.


But today’s puzz?
Bullseye.
👏👏👏

PSA: if you’re about to post something about LAYLOW being the wrong tense (relative to “Tried” in the clue), please don’t.

The entry is correct (“lay” IS past tense in the answer). You’re the one who is wrong.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

TIL SKATE PUNK.
I'll try to listen to a bit of the genre later today.  Once upon a time, I liked PUNK (The Ramones and The Sex Pistols), and the SKATEr sub-culture always seemed cool to me.  
So,...?
We'll see.  
Anyone have recommendations of where to start?

I originally had BAKE for "Veg out in a spa", thinking of my sauna experiences, and that gave me SKATEPANK, which my caffeine-deprived mind assumed was a neo-version of SKA,
but I was able to see my error....eventually.

It took me a minute to understand why CUKEs were vegging out in a spa.
Oh, I get it now.
They say that the veg slices reduce eye-puffiness.
Maybe it's time I give them a try.

Maybe I'm the only one?  Don't see any other comments about it...but I was stuck on what could be filled in for "RET_   _EMENT  P_AN" for a long time before realizing that those first "two" words shouldn't be separated, and it was just an auto line break in an unfortunate spot.
Thanks to The Bloodhound Gang’s “Foxtrot Uniform Charlie Kilo”, I’ll never forget those letters in the NATO alphabet :)

The creativity behind puzzle concepts and construction continues to amaze me. 
However...
All the olds on here with vision challenges, Hands Up
Not a constructor and don't pretend to be one, but maybe the more user-friendly solution to the bold problem was to describe them as "big bold" and use CAPS

The B in GIBE was a bit of a guess for me, as I wasn't entirely sure what I was looking at on the down word. Maybe it was because my caffeine hasn't fully kicked in or maybe I just refuse to learn French terms on principle.

I expect the two most common topics in today's forum will be: 1) Lie LOW vs. LAY LOW--C'mon, Peoples! Weren't you paying attention in Mrs. Peterson's high school English classes?!? 2) MT. DOOM w/o an abbr. in the clue--well, in the text of the LOTR, J.R.R. Tolkien uses the full name, "Mount Doom," throughout, except when he uses "Orodruin" or "the Mountain" (capitalized). But "Mt. Doom" does appear on Christopher Tolkien's end-maps. Nonetheless, as Sam sang to Frodo in Disney's musical adaptation, *Incinerated*, "Let it go!" Looks like no one has ever bothered to arrange the Skye Boat Song for oboe:-( So, here's a version sung by Amanda Powell, backed by the skatepunk band Big Red and Her Bons Amis (aka Apollo's Fire). Fast forward to 1:50 to get past the blather and on to the music; and 5:50 for a few clips of excellent dulcimer-player Tina Bergmann: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BlO9zzMRYyU&list=RDBlO9zzMRYyU&start_radio=1
A Wednesday puzzle without a Rebus is a good day. This was fun but challenging.I got stuck at times but figured out all the clues.

Well... quite a workout for me, but a cute theme and managed to work it out from the crosses. Was a bit surprised to see that all of the theme answers were debuts, but... no big deal. And... puzzle find today. A Sunday from January 16, 1977 by Jordan S. Lasher with the title "Variations." Some theme answers in that one: FEATHERSOFABIRD PARTOFTHEFIRSTPARTY ACTIONOFTHEPIECE MOMENTOFTHESPUR THEMARCHOFIDES COLOROFANOTHERHORSE AHANDINTHEBIRD Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=1/16/1977&g=135&d=A See you tomorrow. ....

I'm stopping by on my way to the Answer Key to check my solve.  MOST of this worked out once I went back to the PC and printed the Newspaper Version.  (Usually if there is an essential feature, the puzzle will ONLY print the version that includes the special features, but not today.  Thanks a lot.)
I know I got all of the Themers, but there are a couple of somewhat baffling entry/clue pairs ...or maybe just one.  
(I am never going to read LOTR, and you can't make me.  And it's too late for me to study French or explore things like 18D.  DHubby is having me learn to use Quicken....that's enough New Tricks for the nonce.)

Looking at his Times bio, I noticed that @Isaac Aronow is an alum of Hampshire College. I wonder what he thinks about the recent news of the shuttering of his alma mater? https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/14/us/hampshire-college-closing-amherst-massachusetts-enrollment.html

I liked the theme today, gotta love themes that deliver the “Aha” moment.

The part at the end I got stuck was crossing GIBE with BONSAMIS.  I’ve never heard of the word GIBE and don’t speak French.  And that foreign language word was crossed with yet another foreign language word.

This was fun.  A playful puzzle that, even though easy, gave me plenty to do as a solver -- which always makes me happy.  After putting together the boldface letters, you then have to find the in-the-language phrase that is its equivalent.  And they were all in-the-language for me -- with the exception of BUILD A BEAR.  Which I'm sure is in-the-language too -- it's just that I've never heard it and have no idea what it means.  So I'll go now and look it up on the blog and in the comments.

A nice breezy puzzle that was very enjoyable.
The bold letters are not visible in the app.

Good morning! Was interested to see whether BUILDABEAR and ROTOROOTER would be too US-centric, but I searched the comments and didn't find any squawking. Yaay! 
TROTS & TROTSKY? Interesting.
I'm already complaining about warmer weather - it's only in the 60's-70's here, I know, I know. The TV tells me I am supposed to be thrilled. But...bah!

Did the puzzle last night. Liked it. Found it well constructed and well edited. Read the column last night. Sigh.

-30-
GIBE / BONSAMIS / NINO / ERNO was no bueno.
I subscribe to online puzzles and none of the "theme" clues had bold letters. It's very easy to do that but someone didn't review the online version.

Brilliant puzzle.

But regarding MTDOOM, is it not a rule that an answer containing an abbreviation must have an abbreviation indicator in the clue? Or do I have that wrong?
Sum41 am my favorit skate punk band. BWAHAHAHAHAHAA

Although t I easily got the answer to 44 down, I am curious. Frodo’s destination is Mount Doom. If we are going to abbreviate it, then shouldn’t the clue have an abbreviation? Maybe Frodo’s destination in LOTR? Or am I being too picky? Just asking.
When you confuse ibex and ibis... 🤦‍♂️

I do like a VODKATONIC on a hot day, and it's supposed to hit 90 degrees, so I'll be having a few. After cutting the grass, of course.

I must have missed that episode of "Mythbusters." Why would you do that to yummy cheese?

Last night as I was solving, I was thinking it was the Thursday puzzle and muttering to myself that this was the easiest Thursday trick yet. Then the light dawned. Nevermind, said someone somewhere once. 

 No trouble seeing the bold letters in dark mode on Android app. Except i did miss the h at the end of the last clue, and for a moment thought, Oh God, will I be clutching pearls? Fortunately, not. 

As for Mordor v Mt Doom,  as someone once said, "Use the crosses Luke." I already had ODE, so Mordor was out. Miss you, Deb.
TROTSKY appears in the puzzle on the 97th anniversary of his arrival in exile in Turkey. Now that's puzzlemaking!
Disappointed today...
Also couldn’t distinguish all the bold letters, CAPs would have been better

Absolutely despised this puzzle. First, as many have already said, the bold letter was nearly impossible to distinguish. This just further is my belief that for the most part, the editors don’t really pay attention to the slop they are printing. Secondly, this puzzle should never have been published any day other than Thursday - Saturday. Lastly, the theme is weak. Once the puzzle is complete. MAKE, CREATE, FORM, and BUILD make the endings make sense with the bold letters but that doesn’t help you after you solve it. 

Rating:
1/10
No bold letters in the print out version.
Once and for all I really need to memorize Mr. Rubik’s first name
Does anyone else think 45 down should’ve used the present tense of “try” instead of “tried”? The clue makes me think the answer should be “laid low”.

Enough with the AROD clues. He's nobody's favorite Yankee, best left forgotten. 

How about... "Homer's nuclear take away"? 

Or... "Bomber that pairs well with juice"?
Way too hard for Wednesday.   I'm not the best solver, but I usually can manage Wednesday slowly.  This one was impossible.   Forget it.  You  wasted my time.
NOFX and Pennywise are both Political Punk, like Anti-Flag. Not skate punk...
No real skater listened to Blink-182. They are a pop punk ripoff of NOFX.
I’m blanking.  Why is 40a CUKE.  I’m sure when I find out I will feel foolish.

I'm back home, and doing the puzzles again.

This was a nice puzzle, solved without the theme. Why the heck can't the Games staff  ensure that the Standard Layout  print includes the bold letters from the Newspaper Version? So annoying.

I’d like to make a bold statement based on this puzzle: 

I saw all the indicated letters in the theme clues without squinting, and I have never in my life incorrectly said “laid low” for the past tense of “lie low”. 
Which in both cases is a bold-faced lay.

(I’ve also never said either of “bald-faced lie” or “bold-faced lie” with 100% confidence.)
What a wonderful puzzle!

Fun puzzle with an engaging theme (quibbles with “SKATEPUNK” notwithstanding). Cute crossing at BONSAMIS and ABREAST, too. I do agree with others that in the app at least, and with my middle-aged eyes, the bolding was difficult to discern.

I enjoyed it, and my solve time was just a hair faster than my Wednesday average so while it was maybe a bit harder than recent Wednesdays I still think it ran on the right day--not tricksy enough for a Thursday, and too easy for a Friday. I do wish someone had noticed that the bolding was missing from most of the print versions and difficult to see on some platforms; I wasn't affected, but it must have been exasperating to those who were. I found a fun Sunday puzzle from 2016 if anyone is looking for additional diversion: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2016/09/04 also I went down the EDAM rabbit hole and posted about it in a response: https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4fecs4?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share

Had to work today. Didn't find this easy, though I got to it later than usual and might have been a bit worn out.

BUILDABEAR means nothing to me. And I really wanted to "forge" and not FORMANALLIANCE.

Seemed like a solid Wednesday puzzle to me.

This was probably my favorite early week puzzle in a while... Maybe so far this year! Finding 4 interesting entries from the same set of words? A well-executed and tight theme, and so many fun clues throughout (looking at you, CUKE). 
I'm very proud of myself for knowing TROTSKY immediately 🙌🏼 His story always fascinated me. And of course any puzzle with MT DOOM and MEOW is fine by me. Thanks, Adam!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ put all the people together in a love cannon
put in all the gold all the paper all the cheese
put in all the wind instruments
put in all the cats
put in the musical, cats
all all together in the cannon together
d/ now the moment of truth
a/ go time
Enjoyed the puzzle, but on my iPad in the NYT Games app, the bold letters were nearly indistinguishable from the non-bold. Need to find a better way to display these in future.
Excellent puzzle. Very clever and original clues!

After seeing the show Company Retreat, I didn't hesitate with 5D, SIA. I struggled with a few clues but managed to get most. I need to commit to memory ERNO and embrace Lord of the Rings culture. A particular clue that I really liked was GOTIME (anytime I am reminded of Seinfeld is always good).

The BOLD didn't show at all on my android phone in dark mode.   It wasn't until I finished the entire puzzle without seeing any BOLD letters that I read sometimes comments that said switching from dark mode to regular mode allowed them to see it better.     I never would have thought of this on my own, so I'm the future the NYT might want to make a note on the intro page to the days puzzle starting such a thing.    Secondly, even in regular mode, the bills letters were hard to discern on my phone.      It is no big deal since most NYT crosswords come off without a hitch on the app, but it took a little fun out of this puzzle for me, since on those long answers I was literally taking a stab in the DARK (pun intended 😃)

I understand this is an Adam Wagner crossword and there are always some quirks in them, but using bonsamis and rotorooter was a bit too extra. These entries were jutting out like a sore thumb in an otherwise good Wednesday.

There's a lot of discussion here about lay/lie. I remember this discussion from high school. I think I would have to look it up--if it came up in important writing I was doing.

Also, from high school, there was a long conversation about the verbs effect/affect and how to use which one where. Ditto. I pretend I didn't go to high school when this comes up.

The construction that really bugs me is:
"A driver has to come to a full stop at a stop sign. Then they have to know which car in a four-way stop has the right of way."

I think you have to say: "A driver has to come to a full stop at a stop sign. Then he or she has to know....etc."

Or you can say: Drivers have to come to a full stop at a stop sign. Then they have to know....etc."

The New York Times has used the first (I believe) incorrect construction. I've seen it!!! Really!!!

As far as knowing which car has the right of way, I'd probably have to look that up, too, since it's been so long since I've driven a car...

I might add that when I worked as an ESL teacher, the feeling was, most of the time, if I understand what you're saying, it's a go. I believe that my students didn't like to focus on the niceties too much!!

"We is going..." Okay. Gotcha.
I'm BONETIRED but I still want to say that I really enjoyed this puzzle and the clever theme. Hopefully, the BONSAMIS are walking ABREAST to share a couple of VODKATONICS.

Got hung up on 6A 8D. Although familiar with gibe, I don't recall ever seeing it spelled. Would have thought it started with a J.
Even after the solve, I kept seeing two words "Bon Samis". I'm well aware of Paris-Brest as a pastry, and not at all aware of Brest as a city. Paris-Brest is a sandwich, being sliced and filled. Thus, Bon Samis. Good sandwich.
And that double dash in the clue for 6A kept pushing my mind to fill in the blank. Sure, quotation marks and e.g., but still the brain wants what he brain wants.
https://youtu.be/J8VjdCAg5Bk?si=YUwmTXXUKUm2CXUZ There will be a quiz afterward.
Could not discern the BOLD letters in the clues on a Chromebook. Made it tough GoInG.
Wow that was a rough one for me.  Just don't understand may of the clues.

Another late puzzle find. A Wednesday from February 23, 2011 by Barry C. Silk. Just don't recall another theme quite like this one. Three theme answers, straightforwardly clued: CHANTILLYLACE CHAINLINKFENCE CHURCHOFFRANCE And then, the reveal... "Remote possibility... or hint to 20-, 29- and 44- across:" OUTSIDECHANCE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/23/2011&g=29&d=A ...

One more puzzle find. It's late so I might link this again tomorrow. Anyway - a Wednesday from February 23, 2011 by Barry C. Silk. Three straightforwardly clued theme answers: CHANTILLYLACE CHAINLINKFENCE CHURCHOFFRANCE And the 'reveal' "Remote possibility... or a hint to 20-, 29- and 44-across:" OUTSIDECHANCE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/23/2011&g=20&d=A ....

Very wide spread of countries involved today. Some extremely American answers mixed with French, Spanish and Italian words, a Scottish island, Indian politician, a Russian, a Hungarian, British singer, Aussie slang, Greek god.
Cutesy (groan) with a number of very vague (being kind here) clues. Tiresome and tedious.

A challenge for me and I took several runs at it, finishing late in the day. But I enjoyed the challenge and I thank you Adam Wagner, for the fun and the challenge too.
There were a number of things that were not in my wheelhouse. Did not know ARLO Parks (much more familiar with Arlo Guthrie). That particular Indian PM was new to me also, and I was sure the Yankee nickname must be "Babe" so that spot took awhile.
Lots of fun though, and I know I'll be seeing more of your puzzles sooner or later. Yay!
A fun Wednesday, thanks

Pretty heady for a Wed., but a very satisfying solve in the end.   Thanks Adam...always enjoy your work (and the challenge).
There is an error in tense in this puzzle. 45 Down - the past tense of the clue requires that the answer should be in the past tense - “laid low” not “lay low”.

@Selective Walrus
The past tense of LIE is LAY. Clue is correct.

@Selective Walrus
According to my 1973 edition of the Random House College Dictionary, bought when I went to college that year, the past tense of the intransitive verb “lie” is “lay”. And I’m not a grammarian, but isn’t lie in this case intransitive?  BTW, I don’t know if it would be taught in high school English class, but one way I remember the past participle of the transitive verb “to lay” is that the expression is “to get laid”, not “to get lain”.
@Whatsername Ah yes, I can hear it now. It’s tense that has fallen out of fashion a bit in modern days, but I get it. You are technically correct, the best kind of correct. :)

@Al  in Pittsburgh  I am an English teacher in Germany, and I have been teaching this lesson for many years.  It can be done!  Now, if Americans would stop leading my poor German students astray by posting things like "Here I am laying on the beach in my new bikini", my life would be a lot easier.  For the record:
to lie lie lay lain = liegen (go horizontal)
to lay lay laid laid = legen (put something somewhere)
to lie lie lied lied = lügen (not tell the truth)

@Selective Walrus 

The first definition I saw in the dictionary was "to cause to fall by a blow," which made me think (incorrectly) the clue was wrong. Lay / lie / lied / laid / lain is so confusing!

@Mark DelGiudice

@Abby

The bold letters indicate what needs to be produced in the answer. In 17A, the bold letters spell ODOR, and the expected answer is MAKE A STINK. The column already gave the expected answer to POOH, the bold letters in the clue for 57A. The other two theme answers are clued by BRUTE and PACT and are ways to produce those items.

@Abby

It seems easy but it’s not. First you have to make a short word from bold letters in the same phrase in order; ok maybe not so difficult. Then you have to create natural phrases that all point to making, creating, forming, etc. that’s much harder and these were all very much “in the language”, not forced. And last they have to be symmetrical.
@George LAY LOW is just plain wrong.  Will, time to retire?

@George

As mentioned earlier, it is correct as clued; LAY is the past tense of LIE, but LAID LOW is also a common version, and is what my answer would have been if the space allowed, which it didn't.

@Steve L

You have made my day with that.  It will be pleasantly stuck in my head!
@George "Brer Fox, he lay low."

@JayTee There is a difference in meaning.  To lie low means to try not to attract attention.  The past tense of lie is lay.  To be laid low means something has put you in a bad way.  It is a passive construction with the verb lay.  The past participle of lay is laid.

@Bruce

Don't bother learning the NATO alphabet.  It won't be in use much longer.  Better to just use the Russian alphabet which will replace it here in the good ol' US of A.

Bruce —In the game of solving crosswords, the long established tradition for what constitutes "cheating" is that everyone sets their own personal standard. Of course outside of competitions nobody really thinks looking something up is cheating. But personally, I like to solve without getting any hints or lookups ... until I can't, and then I do.

@Jacqui J Or as Dylan sang, " Lay Lady Lay."

@SBK in TO

I am on Team Katie on this one. I certainly still use “whom” just to prove I’m educated but grammar does, will, and should change and I won’t miss this one. We get by without a different word for “what”, right? Frankly we get by without different endings for almost all nouns except some pronouns, and probably could live without those (somehow “you” and “you” are ok?) The Greeks had five case endings for every noun, somehow we manage now mostly by word order and context. How about all the Greek verb tenses and modes—ever used aorist, or middle voice lately? They thought they were essential, apparently. Even subjunctive—“If I were a rich man”? If I said “if I was a rich man” would you be confused?
Then there’s just language differences. Lots of languages seem to think gender is essential, we certainly don’t. And lots of languages are grammatically more sparse than ours. Most make do without the article “a” and many without “the”. How about our little “do” in questions like, “Do you want a cookie?” What’s that for? Apparently that’s a holdover from old Celtic and very few other languages have it or need it.
So, if you love the elegance of “whom”, by all means keep it, but just don’t insist that it’s really necessary for understanding.

@Katie You are exactly right about something like lay vs lie. The point of communication is to ... COMMUNICATE (crazy, I know!). If you can clearly get your point across, then the rules don't matter to anyone except a grammarian.

@SP
As the song almost says, you're gonna miss it when it's gone. I happen to know Hebrew which has no word for the present tense of 'to be'. You'd be surprised how confusing it can be not being able to explicitly say 'A is B'. The best you can do is a cave dweller-like 'A B'.

@Francis 
if we all got what we truly deserve... *shudder*

@Francis

You certainly did deserve it!!

@Becky
Me too. I'm praying like crazy for mercy, please, and not justice. I know what I've done and justice is not what I'm looking for.

@SBK in TO

I’m just looking for peace of mind. It’s hard.

For what it's worth, Paris-Brest is a famous cycling event in France between the cities of Paris and Brest. It is older than the Tour de France. It used to be a race but now, I believe, it is a non-competitive event for amateur cyclists. The route is 750 miles long.

How do I know this? I saw famous TV chef Jacques Pépin make an equally famous French dessert called "Paris-Brest." It is made out of fluffy pâte à choux in the shape of a wheel.

Maybe this will help you remember "Brest" for the next puzzle!

I went down the rabbit hole. 
The story of Edam cannonballs refers to an anecdotal, likely apocryphal, tale from the 1841 Uruguayan Civil War. According to the legend, Uruguayan Commodore John Coe (or Co), having run out of traditional ammunition during a naval battle against Argentina, ordered his men to load the cannons with hard, red-waxed balls of Edam cheese. 

The cheese was supposedly effective, tearing the sails and damaging the rigging of the Argentinian ship commanded by Admiral William Brown. The story first appeared in the Aristocratic Monitor in 1847, claiming it came from Coe's anonymous first lieutenant.

While the battle occurred, historical records do not confirm the use of cheese as ammunition.

Edam cheese is uniquely suited to this legend because it was historically produced in hard, spherical shapes and covered in paraffin wax, allowing it to withstand long sea journeys. 

MythBusters tested this story (Episode 129) and found that while aged Edam is hard, it did not act as an effective cannonball, bouncing off sails rather than damaging them.

@Josh @Tim
Sadly you're both incorrect. LAY is the past tense of lie.  Ask a dictionary, Or  as SP suggested, read the many comments already made about it.

@Tim I realize this may be addressed below, but just for fun: LAY LOW is the correct past tense of “lie low,” because it features the intransitive verb “to lie.” I think using “lay low” as a present tense phrase is common but grammatically incorrect.

@Josh

 Got it. Now we’re clear. You are saying that people colloquially say “lay low” in the present even though it’s grammatically suspect, and in that case “laid low” would be the correct past. A cursory review of the phrase suggests that’s an alternate usage. Still, “lie low” is the strictly correct use of the phrase, and plenty of people including myself use it that way. So at best you could say that “lay low” and “laid low” might both be acceptable, but in any case “lay low” is certainly not incorrect.
